name: MinnesotaCare
source: MN Statutes Chapter 256L; MNsure program guidelines
description:
MinnesotaCare is a subsidized health insurance program for Minnesotans who earn too much to qualify for Medical Assistance (Medicaid) but cannot afford private insurance. It provides comprehensive health coverage with income-based monthly premiums that are significantly lower than private insurance costs. MinnesotaCare is a Basic Health Program under the ACA, funded jointly by the state and federal government.

MinnesotaCare covers adults ages 19-64. Household income must be between 138% and 200% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL) to qualify. For 2026: the income window is approximately $1,800-$2,608/month for a single person, or $2,418-$3,505/month for a household of 2, or $3,037-$4,402/month for a household of 3. Income below 138% FPL qualifies for Medical Assistance instead.

Applicants must not have access to affordable employer-sponsored insurance. If an employer offers coverage that meets minimum value standards and costs less than a certain percentage of household income, the applicant may not qualify for MinnesotaCare. People who are eligible for Medicare are not eligible.

Monthly premiums for MinnesotaCare are on a sliding scale based on income and family size. Premiums generally range from approximately $0-$80/month per person depending on income. Copayments are low, similar to MA levels ($1-$6 range per service). The benefit package is comprehensive and includes doctor visits, specialist care, hospital services, mental health and substance use treatment, prescription drugs, and preventive care.

MinnesotaCare is a year-round enrollment program through MNsure. There is no waiting period for most services after enrollment. Enrollees must renew coverage annually and report income changes throughout the year. The program has served hundreds of thousands of Minnesotans since 1992 and is a key bridge between MA and private insurance in the state's coverage continuum.

Children in households that exceed MinnesotaCare income limits may still be eligible for Medical Assistance or the Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP), which in Minnesota is administered through MA.
eligibility_summary: Ages 19-64. Income between 138% and 200% FPL (2026: $1,800-$2,608/month for 1 person; $2,418-$3,505 for 2; $3,037-$4,402 for 3). No access to affordable employer coverage. Not eligible for Medicare. Minnesota resident. U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen.
benefit_amounts: Monthly premiums: sliding scale, approximately $0-$80/person/month. Low copays ($1-$6 per service). Full coverage: primary care, specialist, hospital, mental health, prescriptions, dental for some enrollees.
documents_required: Proof of identity, Social Security number, proof of Minnesota residency, proof of income (pay stubs, tax returns), documentation of any employer insurance offer (if applicable), immigration status documents if applicable.
